coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of yellow emissive carbon dots by mild heating process and their utility as fluorescent test paper for detection of mercury(II) ions assistants by smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yunqi
Song, Ying
Li, Tingting
Tuo, Yuzhuo
Tian, Miaomiao
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: The security of food and drink water is of great concern in our daily life, because the threat from heavy metals pollution in environment is extensively existed. Carbon dots (CDs) is promising for a wide range of applications because of its excellent chemical and optical properties. Herein, yellow emissive carbon dots (YCDs) were constructed by a facile mild condition. The YCDs emitted bright emission at 562 nm with excitation of 420 nm and the quantum yield of 17.44%. The fluorescence of YCDs can be utilized of sensing Hg2+ with limit of 16.8 nM. The mechanism of detection for Hg2+ can be attributed to static quenching effect (SQE) and dynamic quenching effect (DQE) process. A fluorescent portable paper detection platform based on YCDs integrated with a smartphone APP were developed for real-time and rapid analysis of Hg2+ with limit of 0.11 μM. Notably, YCDs were successfully employed to the detection of Hg2+ in various food and real water with recoveries of 97.50 ± 2.63% – 108.64 ± 0.15%, indicated satisfied sensing performance and excellent environmental tolerance. The fluorescence sensing system based on YCDs provided a reference for the construction of portable sensors for hazardous substance monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Sensing
Paper sensor
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection for Hg2+ in real samples
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation, Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation , Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciated the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22171060 ), Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University ( HSDSSCX2022–14 ), Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation , Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students ( 201810231009 ), Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation , Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students ( S202210231019 ).

The authors appreciated the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060), Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2022–14), Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation, Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students (201810231009), Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation, Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students (S202210231019).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of YCDs, (inset: HRTEM image), (b) corresponding size distribution. (c) FT-IR spectra of OPD, Gl and YCDs. High-resolution spectra of C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e) and O 1 s (f). (g) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission of YCDs, insert: images of YCDs under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right). (h) The emission with excitation wavelength of 380–480 nm. (i) The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of YCDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of YCDs with 100 μM of various metal ions, (inset: corresponding images under UV lamp). (b) Effects of different ions and coexistence of different ions with Hg2+ on FL intensity of YCDs. (c) paper sensors sensing performance under UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence emission of YCDs mixed with various concentrations (0–100 μM) of Hg2+. Inset: relevant images under UV lamp. (b) Continuous dot plots at 562 nm. Inset: the linear relationship between the emission ratio (I/I0) of YCDs and the concentrations of Hg2+ (0.4 μM–1.0 μM). (c) Fitted curve of color ratio value (G/B) analyzed by smartphone Color Grab APP.

(a) FL lifetime of YCDs and YCDs+Hg2+. (b) UV–vis absorption of Hg2+ and FL excitation and emission spectra of YCDs. (c) The UV–vis absorption of YCDs, Hg2+ and YCDs+Hg2+. Inset: corresponding UV–vis absorption at 350–500 nm. (d) FT-IR spectra of YCDs and YCDs+Hg2+.
Illustration of the preparation of YCDs, their application in detection of Hg2+ (0 μM–100 μM), and exploring to test paper for visual sensing Hg2+ and recognization by smartphone.
The rate of recovery of Hg2+ in samples (Using fluorescent probe and paper sensor, n = 3).

head.ce:title: Controllable synthesis of yellow emissive carbon dots by mild heating process and their utility as fluorescent test paper for detection of mercury(II) ions assistants by smartphone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yunqi Hao and Ying Song contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The security of food and drink water is of great concern in our daily life, because the threat from heavy metals pollution in environment is extensively existed. Carbon dots (CDs) is promising for a wide range of applications because of its excellent chemical and optical properties. Herein, yellow emissive carbon dots (YCDs) were constructed by a facile mild condition. The YCDs emitted bright emission at 562 nm with excitation of 420 nm and the quantum yield of 17.44%. The fluorescence of YCDs can be utilized of sensing Hg2+ with limit of 16.8 nM. The mechanism of detection for Hg2+ can be attributed to static quenching effect (SQE) and dynamic quenching effect (DQE) process. A fluorescent portable paper detection platform based on YCDs integrated with a smartphone APP were developed for real-time and rapid analysis of Hg2+ with limit of 0.11 μM. Notably, YCDs were successfully employed to the detection of Hg2+ in various food and real water with recoveries of 97.50 ± 2.63% – 108.64 ± 0.15%, indicated satisfied sensing performance and excellent environmental tolerance. The fluorescence sensing system based on YCDs provided a reference for the construction of portable sensors for hazardous substance monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my data/code at the attach file step.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is one of the most poisonous heavy metal elements, which has become a serious threat to human health due to its contaminative possibility in the food chain and water [1,2]. Long-term exposure to mercury ion (Hg2+) can permanently damage human organs and cause neurological and immune system disorders, even causing serious illnesses such as Minamata disease [3,4]. Worst still, a trace amount of Hg2+ can cause severe injuries due to its poor degradability and strong transfer capability. The World Health Organization (WHO) [5] has stipulated the permitted Hg2+ in drinkable water was 30 nM. Therefore, it is essential to develop a method that can monitor Hg2+ in the environment water on-site.

At present, conventional technologies involving atomic absorption spectroscopy [6], electrochemical analysis [7], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [8], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [9] are used to monitor Hg2+ with excellent effect. However their defects such as complicated processes and reliance on large appliance, resulted in high cost and complex operation, which make them unavailable for rapid on-site detection. Thus, it is crucial to explore efficient and practical method for visual analysis and rapid sensing of Hg2+, to meet the need of routine monitor in food and water environment [10]. Recently, fluorescent probes have been developed due to their superiority of excellent sensing performance, convenient implementation and real time, in-situ [11,12]. The fluorescent probe based on quantum dots (QDs) [13], metal/covalent organic frameworks (MOFs and COF) [14], gold/sliver nanoclusters (Au/Ag NCs) [15] have been constructed for Hg2+ detection, and gained great achievement. However, their disadvantages such as high toxicity [16] and high cost limited their practical application. Therefore, the fabrication of environment friendly fluorescent nanoprobes are urgent needed with safe, low cost, and high sensitivity. Carbon dots (CDs), as novel and promising luminescent material, have attracted much attention due to their low toxicity, facile fabrication process and excellent optical properties, and most important the low cost carried much more opportunity to CDs [17]. Possessing abundant functional groups on the surface of carbon cores, provided active sites to coordinate with chemicals, which allowed CDs employing as probe to detect various heavy metals. CDs have been widely reported to detect various heavy metals. For instance, Wang et al. synthesized SCDs by hydrothermal method, and utilized to recognize Hg2+ in real water samples and grape juice [18]. Although CDs could be synthesized using a wide range of carbon resources by different methods, it is a gigantic challenge to fabricate CDs controllably via the conformation sensitive to environmental changes.

To date, extensive synthetic approaches, including top-down and bottom-up methods, have been pursued to produce C-dots. Among them, the hydrothermal and microwave subordinated to bottom-up method were the most common for the synthesis of CDs, and the heating method was less reported [19–21]. But the synthesis of heating process can fabricate CDs with high QY by tuning and monitoring their reaction conditions conveniently. And the corresponding application was also affected by their properties and surface functional groups, because the recognition group determined the optical properties and sensing selectivity for different analytes, and the signal response converted the identification signal into a fluorescent signal, thereby achieving target analyte detection [22,23].

To achieve the controllable synthesis for the appropriate fluorescent probe, the ratio of precursors, temperature, reaction time should be evaluated by estimating their effect of factors on the nucleation and growth of nanocrystals [24]. Therefore, to controllably investigate and optimize the synthesis of CDs in details, the conventional heating method was highly recommended. And also, except tuning their optical properties and surface functional groups, this method is also benefit to obtain high emissive CDs with large scale product. In recent years, more and more portable devices have been developed [25]. The simple, time-saving, cost-effective production of CDs and their amazing reactivity with various metal ions make them a strong candidate for metal ion detection. However, the limitations such as the portability of CDs solution-based fluorescent probes, it is of great significance to develop a cost-effective portable sensing device in combination with carbon dots. Moreover, test strips have been developed widely by assembling CDs to test paper for portable analysis, which exhibited great advantages of low cost, portable feasibility, and easy operation [26,27]. Further, smartphone supported APP can be applied to detect target analyte by recognizing fluorescent color as a platform, which can offer rapid and accuracy sensing, provide a high effective portable method for on-site detection [28,29]. For example, Zhang et al. developed a CDs integrated paper sensor for in-situ visual detection for glyphosate [30]. Inspired by this, the fluorescent smartphone platform would be an outstanding portable analytical unit device, which exhibited promising potential application in the field of efficient detection.

Herein, yellow fluorescent CDs were fabricated using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and glycerol (Gl) as raw materials by a facile one step heating process ( Scheme 1). By tuning the ratio of precursors and conditions, the obtained YCDs emitted yellow fluorescence with QY of 17.44%, and lifetime of 2.58 μs. The YCDs could be employed as probe to tracing Hg2+ with promptly quenching fluorescence. Remarkably, the detecting behavior can be developed to the paper-based sensors readily. By impregnating the YCDs on filter paper, a low-cost portable yellow fluorescent paper sensor can be constructed and performed conveniently. Further, integrating with smartphone APP, the YCDs paper sensor was explored as quantitative and on-site sensing system to monitor Hg2+ effectively. The facile synthesized YCDs and corresponding test paper exhibited great virtues of rapid, readable and accuracy detection, which endowed YCDs promising application in monitoring Hg2+ in food and water resource.

YCDs was constructed by a rarely reported straightforward heating reaction. In details, 0.05 g of OPD and 1 mL of Gl were poured into a round-bottom flask with stirring. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 1 h by an oil bath. The brown sticky product was dissolved in a round-bottomed flask by 5 mL ultrapure water after cooling to ambients temperature. The obtained solution was purified by filtering via a 0.22 µm filter membrane, and further dialyzed with ultrapure water in a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000) for 24 h. Finally, the dark brown powders were collected by freeze-drying for further use. To optimize the synthesis of YCDs, different conditions including temperature, reaction time and ratio of reactants was carried out in this work (the details in Supporting Information).

The fabrication of YCDs based fluorescent paper sensor was a simple process by immersing the filter paper strips in YCDs solution. The filter paper was sheared to the same size (5 mm × 5 mm) firstly, then immersed in the diluted 50 times YCDs solution for 10 min. The YCDs based paper sensor was obtained by taking out soaked filter paper by tweezers and dried in air.

The diluted 100 times YCDs solution by ultrapure water was employed as a fluorescent probe in this work. For estimating the selectivity, fourteen cations (including Hg2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+ and Mn2+) were picked for studying their effect on the fluorescence of YCDs. The related interference of detection was also evaluated by testing the fluorescence of 100 μM of relevant ions samples with YCDs, and further with addition of Hg2+ (volume ratio=1: 1). To test the sensitivity, different concentrations of Hg2+ were mixed with YCDs probe (with volume ratio of 1: 1), and fluorescence of all samples were measured and compared to estimate the FL response of YCDs.

The detection performance of paper sensor was also investigated by testing selectivity and sensitivity in the same way. The solution of ions were dropped on the surface of the test strip, then the FL color of paper was evaluated under UV lamp (365 nm). All FL images of samples were taken under 365 nm UV lamp by a vivo y73s smartphone supported by color Grab APP, which can transfer the FL color information to R, G, and B values (prime optical colors, R: red, G: green, B: blue). The RGB values represented the fluorescent color information can be obtained, which were related to the concentration of Hg2+ tightly, acquired reliable relevance between the RGB and concentration of Hg2+ achieved the convenient detection by smart phone. To ensure the accuracy of detection, the three points were picked on each sample to guarantee and calculate the standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection for Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the sensing performance of YCDs and their paper sensor in real samples, the environmental water and various food samples were assembled and used in the experiments. Sea water (Yellow Sea), lake water (Chagan Lake), tap water (our laboratory), milk (Wahaha AD calcium milk, purchased from campus supermarket), fish (Crucian carp, purchased from local market) and shrimp (River shrimp, purchased from local market) were collected and used as the real samples for the measurement of Hg2+. Fish and shrimp samples were pretreated before use refering to the provious method with slight adjustments [31]. Specifically, 1.0 g of the ground sample was weighed precisely, then soaked in 200 μL concentrated nitric acid and treated by ultrasonication for 10 min. Consecutively, 10 mL of 0.1 M nitric acid was supplied to a total volume of 10 mL, and treated by ultrasonication for 10 min for adequate extraction. Then a fraction of the resulting suspension was separated by centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 15 min. After collecting the supernatant of sample, the food extraction was obtained, and the pH of the product should be adjusted to optimum condition before use. All real samples were filtered and purified by a 0.22 µm microporous membrane firstly, and then spiking with various concentrations of Hg2+ for practical test successively.

The synthesis and optimization of YCDs were performed by study the effects of fluorescence on the temperature, reaction time and the ratio of precursors. The facile process performed by heating OPD with mixing of high boiling point solvent Gl, which was easy implementation and allowed scaling up the synthesis of YCDs. As demonstrated in Fig. S1, the reaction time was firstly evaluated by tuning from 140 °C to 220 °C, in comparison, the fluorescence spectra and the corresponding experimental phenomenon photos illustrated that the optimum reaction time can be determined at 180 °C. Further the reaction time was optimized and assigned at 1 h by comparing their fluorescent intensity and images (Fig. S2). The fluorescence of different ratio of precursors was also surveyed, the 0.05 g OPD with 1 mL of Gl were confirmed as ultimate reaction conditions. Compared with the reported synthetic method [20,21], the hydrothermal and microwave were the most popular, the heating method in this work exhibited controllable tuning and monitoring in the process of synthesis, which facilitate the optical properties and potential application expediently.

The morphology and crystallinity of YCDs was measured by TEM. The TEM image of YCDs ( Fig. 1a) showed the YCDs was spherical with well dispersed state, with mean particles size of 2.1 ± 0.02 nm (Fig. 1b) (based on a statistical analysis of 100 dots). And a well-resolved crystal lattice noted in the HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1a), the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be consistent with the (100) lattice plane of graphene [32], confirmed the synthesis of YCDs.

The chemical composition and surface functional groups of YCDs were further investigated by FT-IR and XPS. As revealed in Fig. 1c, the broadband at 3390 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O−H/N − H groups [33]. The two bands at 1500 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CC/CN, and the peak at 756 cm–1 belonged to the out-plane deformations of aromatic C−H, implying that existence of aromatic clusters. The absorption peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 stemmed from stretching vibration of CO and CC/C−N. The characteristic band of region 1035–1227 cm–1 denoted a stretching vibration groups of C−O−C and C−O [34]. Meanwhile, the significantly difference and diversity of YCDs and reactants in FT-IR further proved the successful synthesis of CDs. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S4) showed the existence of three pronounced peaks at C 1 s (284.8 eV), N 1 s (399.3 eV), and O 1 s (532.6 eV), indicating YCDs contained elements of C, N and O, with their atomic ratios of 80.54%, 10.58%, and 8.88%, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1d) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.64 eV, 285.34 eV, and 286.30 eV, which can be attributed to CC/C−C, C−N /C−O, and C−O−C [35], respectively. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) exhibited the characteristic peaks of pyridinic N, pyrrole N, and graphitic N at 398.74, 399.40, and 400.39 eV [34,36]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1f) presented three peaks were assigned to CO (531.84 eV), C−OH (532.66 eV), and C−O (533.41 eV), indicated the existence of oxygen-rich groups [37]. The various surfacial groups were found in accordance with the analysis of FT-IR, indicated the modification by abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of YCDs, confirmed Gl not only acted as a high-boiling point solvent but also participated in the reaction as a reactant during the construction of CDs, facilitated their good solubility.

The optical properties of YCDs including UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were surveyed to research the optical properties. As portrayed in Fig. 1g, a strong peak at 289 nm was attributed to the π − π * transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [38]. In the lower-energy region, a wide and weak absorption peak located at 420 nm were found and confirmed to the n − π * transition of oxygen-containing or nitrogen-containing functional groups, indicated abundant surface modification of YCDs [39]. The Fig. 1g indicated that the maximum emission at 562 nm under 420 nm excitation, and the optimal excitation and emission were consistent with the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1i). The excitation spectrum overlapped well with absorption curve in the range of 350–500 nm, indicated the surface-related absorption was responsible for the yellow emission. The diluted YCDs aqueous solution exhibited pale yellow color under sunlight, and displayed strong yellow fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1g), manifested the YCDs expressed unique outstanding optical characteristic. Furthermore, the excitation independent behavior confirmed by evaluating the emission with various excitations ( Fig. 2h), which corresponded to the uniform surface states of the YCDs [40]. The FL decay dynamics was approximately monoexponential under the excitation of 420 nm (Fig. 4a), with a lifetime of 2.58 μs, which further proved the only one FL center, namely the surface state, accounted for the emission [41]. The relative quantum yield was calculated as 17.44% by choosing Rhodamine 6G as the reference (the details supplied in Supporting Information).

The optical stability was studied due to its key role as essential factor in sensing, the effect of various complex conditions on the fluorescence intensity of YCDs were displayed in Fig. S5. The pH value exhibited a particular impact on the optical stability of YCDs, the fluorescence intensity exhibited relatively stable within a wide range of pH (5.0 −12.0), which ensured the feasibility of YCDs applicated on complex environment. Moreover, the influence of ionic stress on FL intensity was estimated by mixing with various concentration of NaCl solution (0.2–1.0 M) (Fig. S6), the result showed the FL intensity of YCDs exhibited neglectable change at higher concentration of salt. Additionally, the photobleaching resistance of YCDs was evaluated via exposing YCDs at ultraviolet light of 365 nm for 20 min, the nondestructive FL intensity proved the high photostability and excellent photobleaching resistance of YCDs (Fig. S7). The FL intensity of YCDs remained almost no change after 30 days of storage (Fig. S8). All these results confirmed YCDs possessed excellent stability and high environmental tolerance.

Possessing abundant functional groups and unique optical nature, YCDs were enabled quantitative detecting heavy metal ions because of the special binding relationship. The specificity of YCDs was estimated by reacting with different metal ions. As delineated in Fig. 2a, the FL intensity of the probe at 562 nm reduced sharply with addition of Hg2+, while the FL intensity of YCDs with other metal ions exhibited little change compared with the blank sample. These results can also be observed from the photograph (inset in Fig. 2a), only Hg2+ quenched the emission of YCDs was found, which confirmed their outstanding selectivity for Hg2+. In addition, the recognitional ability of YCDs to Hg2+ was further explored when Hg2+ coexistence with other ions. Compared with different ions samples, the coexistence with other ions could not influence the detection of Hg2+. The results (Fig. 2b) showed the FL of YCDs slumped intuitively with Hg2+ over other ions indicated the coexistence of their relevant metal ion could not restrict the capacity of identification for Hg2+. The excellent sensing behavior can be comfortably duplicated to the utility of paper sensor. As displayed in Fig. 2c, Hg2+ notably quenched the yellow fluorescence of the YCDs-based fluorescent test strip, indicating that paper sensor duplicated the sensing ability for Hg2+ with high selectivity, which manifested the excellent detection behavior of YCDs and paper sensor. The successful preparation of the portable paper sensor provided a more convenient, rapid, and cost-effective probe for the application and expansion of the CDs fluorescent probe.

To further investigate the quantitative analysis of Hg2+, a series of various concentrations of Hg2+ were introduced, and the fluorescence spectra of all samples were measured. As shown in Fig. 3a, the emission increasingly slumped with gradual rise of Hg2+ concentration. The fluorescence quenching phenomenon appeared in the corresponding images of samples taken under the UV lamp (inset in Fig. 3a), which were accord with the results of fluorescence spectra, revealed the FL intensity was dependent on concentration of Hg2+. Thus, the correlation between FL intensity and concentration of Hg2+ can be established, Fig. 3b and inset illustrated excellent linearity with y = − 0.255x + 1.056 in a narrow range (0.4 μM–1.0 μM) between I/I0 and concentrations of Hg2+ (R2 = 0.9918). The limit of detection (LOD) was 16.8 nM deduced according to the expression of 3σ/k [42], which below the maximum survival concentration (30 nM) in drink water regulated by the WHO [5]. Compared with previously reported sensors using various fluorescent materials to detect Hg2+, the sensitivity of this reported is higher or slightly lower than that of other materials (Table S1). And a comparison between the results obtained by the established method based on the YCDs and those achieved by the previously reported methods based on other CDs is depicted in Table S2. The LOD of the as-constructed sensor is comparable with other methods while the developed detection method combining paper sensor and smartphone software greatly improves the timeliness and portability of Hg2+ detection, which is crucial for the detection of Hg2+ in actual samples.

In addition, Fig. S9 displayed that distinct colorimetric transformation of paper sensor could be observed by naked eyes accompanied by rising Hg2+, and fluorescent color of paper sensor changed from yellow to dark blue (the blue color comes from the UV lamp reflected off the test paper after quenching), showing visual distinguishable results. The good readability under UV lamp, and visual semi-quantitative detection by naked eye endowed paper sensor great promising appliable possibility. Because the resolution of the smartphone camera is higher than the naked eye, the R, G and B values of the images were extracted by the smartphone APP to realize the accurate intelligent detection of Hg2+. Fig. S10–15 shows the relationship between R, etc. and Hg2+ concentration, and it is not difficult to find a superior linear relationship between G/B and Hg2+ concentration. As portrayed in Fig. 3c, there are two excellent relevance between G/B and Hg2+ concentration. The linear equation of G/B with respect to Hg2+ concentration (within the concentration range of 0.1 μM to 20 μM) is y = − 0.0364x + 1.754 (R2 = 0.998), the LOD was calculated as 0.11 μM, and in the concentration range of 20 μM to 100 μM, the linear equation of G/B with respect to Hg2+ concentration is y = − 0.00962x + 1.219 (R2 = 0.996). The above results indicated that the YCDs fluorescence test paper developed in this study can selectively identify Hg2+. The application of smart phones simplified the analytical equipment and enabled rapid visual detection of Hg2+ in the field. This not only provided a simple and effective method for Hg2+ monitoring, but also offered a new idea for developing new detection methods, which exhibited great potential in future practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Possessing excellent sensing performance, the monitoring Hg2+ by YCDs and paper sensor in real water and various food was further evaluated. In order to verify the practical application for Hg2+ determination, three kinds of water samples (i.e., tap water, lake water, and sea water), and three kinds of food samples (i.e., milk, fish, and shrimp) were collected, the applicable detecting was performed by spiking a certain concentration of Hg2+ into water and food samples, the obtained detection results were displayed in Table 1. The recoveries of Hg2+ acquired by the two probes were within the range of 97.50 ± 2.63% – 108.64 ± 0.15%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were between 0.14% and 5.81%, manifested excellent performance of the fluorescent probe and paper sensor sensing platform in real samples. These results proved the excellent practicable performance and environmental tolerance of YCDs and paper sensor, manifested their promising practical application value.

The prospective sensing mechanism was further explored by estimating the lifetime of samples. Generally, the fluorescence quenching caused by static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or a combination of both [43]. As displayed in Fig. 4a, the average fluorescence lifetime of YCDs without and with Hg2+ was 2.58 μs and 1.66 μs, respectively. The result with Hg2+ was found to be eliminated 35.66%, manifested the quenching YCDs was a DQE process preliminarily. To accurately elaborate the quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching of YCDs was described at various concentrations of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S16 and Fig. S17, the Stern-Volmer plot fitted a nonlinear equation within the concentration range of 0.4 μM to 80 μM, among which it was linear at 0.4 μM to 1 μM while displayed an upward deviation at higher concentrations. It suggested that fluorescence quenching was not caused by one mechanism [44]. Furthermore, the low concentration of Hg2+ (0.4 – 1 μM) was described by the Stern-Volmer equation with good linear behavior, and KSV was 3.50 × 105 M−1, and Kq was further calculated as 1.36 × 1011 M−1s−1, which was greater than 2.0 × 1010 M−1s−1, further confirmed that quenching of YCDs by Hg2+ can be explained by the SQE process [45]. Therefore, it can be inferred that both DQE and SQE may be existed in the fluorescence quenching process.
I0/I= 1 +KSV[Q]= 1 +Kqτ0[Q].

I0 and I represent fluorescence intensity of 562 nm without and with Hg2+. KSV represents quenching constant, [Q] delegates the concentration of Hg2+, Kq designates the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is set to the FL lifetime of YCDs.

As portrayed in Fig. 4b, no overlap was found from the absorption spectra of Hg2+ and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra was found, indicated fluorescence quenching could not be caused by the Internal filter effect (IFE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [23]. In addition, the absorption of YCDs, Hg2+ and YCDs+Hg2+ were also studied as displayed in Fig. 4c. A novel peak at 450 nm can be found from UV–vis spectra of YCDs+Hg2+ compared to Hg2+ and YCDs. The FT-IR spectra of YCDs with or without Hg2+ were also performed and the pattern was illustrated in Fig. 4d, in which the broad peak of the N − H at around 3100 cm−1 became stronger, while the characteristic peak of CC/C−N tensile (1384 cm−1) became wider and stronger after the addition of Hg2+. The results speculated that the binding of Hg2+ to functional groups in YCDs caused the generation of new nonfluorescent or weak fluorescent complex [46]. The TEM image of YCDs+Hg2+ expressed serious aggregation further confirmed this speculation (Fig. S18) [47]. Based on the previous analysis, the FL quenching mechanisms of YCDs for Hg2+ detection originated from the synergistic effect of DQE and SQE, which formed the Hg2+-CDs complex via the coordination of chemical bonds, and triggered the promoting fluorescent signal turning off.

In this work, YCDs with bright yellow fluorescence was optimized and fabricated by a facile heating process. Based on YCDs a fluorescent probe and paper sensor for Hg2+ detection were developed and evaluated. The detection system responded sensitively to Hg2+, in which the yellow emission of YCDs quenched by the interaction between Hg2+ and the YCDs, and a combination of DQE and SQE. The LOD of the fluorescent probe for Hg2+ was 16.8 nM, and the lowest Hg2+ detectable by the paper sensor was 1 μM by naked eyes. More importantly, the paper sensor combined with the smartphone APP with LOD of 0.11 μM, emboding potential as convenient visual analysis terminal for quantitative Hg2+ tracing in real foods and water sample. The development of detection platforms based on YCDs will provide new prospect for the application of novel fluorescence sensors in real samples in the future.

Yunqi Hao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ying Song: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Tingting Li: Methodology, Investigation. Yuzhuo Tuo: Data curation, Investigation. Miaomiao Tian: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Fang Chai: Funding acquisition, Visualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciated the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060), Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2022–14), Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation, Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students (201810231009), Department of Heilongjiang Province Innovation, Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students (S202210231019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.109863.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
